coredata.dc:title: Comparative study of the carbon nanofilm and nanodots grown by plasma-enhanced hot filament chemical vapor deposition
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, B.B.
Chen, C.C.
Zheng, K.
Cheng, Q.J.
Wang, L.
Wang, R.Z.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanofilm and nanodots were grown by plasma-enhanced hot filament chemical vapor deposition using methane, hydrogen and nitrogen as the reactive gases. The results of field emission scanning electron microscopy, micro-Raman spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy indicate that the amorphous carbon nanofilm and nanodots are formed without and with nitrogen, respectively. The formation of carbon nanofilm and nanodots is the consequence of different sputtering-etching effects. The photoluminescence (PL) of carbon nanofilm and nanodots was studied in a SPEX 1403 Ramalog system using a 325nm He–Cd laser as an excitation source and the PL spectra show the PL bands centered at about 411 and 513nm for the carbon nanofilm and 405 and 504nm for the carbon nanodots. Simultaneously, the PL results also indicate that the intensity of PL bands of carbon nanofilm is lower than that of carbon nanodots. The generation of different PL bands was interpreted by the transition mechanism. The difference in the intensity of PL bands is related to the size of carbon nanodots. The electron field emission (EFE) characteristics of carbon nanofilm and nanodots were investigated in a high-vacuum system. The results show that Fowler–Nordhelm curves are composed of two or three straight lines and the carbon nanofilm can emit a high current density, which originate from the diversification of carbon nanodots. The difference in the EFE results of carbon nanofilm and nanodots is associated to the size and number of carbon nanodots. These results can enrich our knowledge about carbon-based nanomaterials and are important to fabricate the carbon-based solid nanodevices in the field of optoelectronics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanofilm
Carbon dots
Photoluminescence
Electron field emission

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structures and composition of carbon nanofilm and nanodots
The formation of carbon nanofilm and nanodots
Photoluminescence of carbon nanofilm and nanodots
EFE properties of carbon film and nanodots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FESEM images of (a) carbon film and (b) carbon nanodots.
Raman spectra of (a) carbon film and (b) carbon nanodots.
XPS spectra of (a) carbon film and (b) carbon nanodots.
C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s core level XPS spectra of specimens A and B.
PL spectra of (a) carbon film and (b) carbon dots.
Fitted Raman spectra of (a) A and (b) B specimens.
J–E curves of (a) carbon film and (b) carbon dots. The insets are the corresponding F–N curves.
Size distribution of carbon nanodots in Fig. 1(b).

The flow rates of CH4, H2 and N2, growth temperature T

b , bias U
b , and growth time t.
The content of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen in the specimens A and B.
The position, full width at half maximum (FWHM) and intensity of peaks fitted for the Raman spectra in Fig. 2.

head.ce:title: Comparative study of the carbon nanofilm and nanodots grown by plasma-enhanced hot filament chemical vapor deposition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University of Technology, 69 Hongguang Road, Lijiatuo, Banan District, Chongqing 400054, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University of Technology, 69 Hongguang Road, Lijiatuo, Banan District

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing 100124, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Beijing University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Microstructure and Properties of Advanced Materials, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing 100124, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Microstructure and Properties of Advanced Materials, Beijing University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy Research, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanofilm and nanodots were grown by plasma-enhanced hot filament chemical vapor deposition using methane, hydrogen and nitrogen as the reactive gases. The results of field emission scanning electron microscopy, micro-Raman spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy indicate that the amorphous carbon nanofilm and nanodots are formed without and with nitrogen, respectively. The formation of carbon nanofilm and nanodots is the consequence of different sputtering-etching effects. The photoluminescence (PL) of carbon nanofilm and nanodots was studied in a SPEX 1403 Ramalog system using a 325nm He–Cd laser as an excitation source and the PL spectra show the PL bands centered at about 411 and 513nm for the carbon nanofilm and 405 and 504nm for the carbon nanodots. Simultaneously, the PL results also indicate that the intensity of PL bands of carbon nanofilm is lower than that of carbon nanodots. The generation of different PL bands was interpreted by the transition mechanism. The difference in the intensity of PL bands is related to the size of carbon nanodots. The electron field emission (EFE) characteristics of carbon nanofilm and nanodots were investigated in a high-vacuum system. The results show that Fowler–Nordhelm curves are composed of two or three straight lines and the carbon nanofilm can emit a high current density, which originate from the diversification of carbon nanodots. The difference in the EFE results of carbon nanofilm and nanodots is associated to the size and number of carbon nanodots. These results can enrich our knowledge about carbon-based nanomaterials and are important to fabricate the carbon-based solid nanodevices in the field of optoelectronics.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon can form different structural materials depending on the sp 1, sp 2 and sp 3 hybridized C–C bonds, thus the carbon-based nanomaterials have been extensively studied in the last decades to meet different applications. For example, graphene, carbon nanotubes, carbon nanotips and nanodots have been synthesized using different methods so that they reveal the potential applications in the fields of microelectronics and optoelectronics [1–6]. In particular, the carbon nanodots can generate strong photoluminescence (PL) and show the excellent photocatalytic properties [7–9], which recently attract significant attention. However, the carbon nanofilms can generate the PL [10,11], is there the difference in the PL behaviors of carbon nanofilms and nanodots? If there is the difference, what is the difference? This activates us to comparatively study on the PL properties of carbon nanofilms and nanodots. Simultaneously, the electron field emission (EFE) properties of carbon nanofilms and nanodots are comparatively studied.

Carbon nanodots have been synthesized by various chemical and physical methods such as electrochemical etching of carbon fibers, hydrothermal synthesis and laser ablation [6,9,12,13]. In this work, the carbon nanofilm and carbon nanodots were synthesized by plasma-enhanced hot filament chemical vapor deposition (PEHFCVD) because the reactive plasma is extensively employed to fabricate namomaterials [14,15]. It is interesting to find that the carbon nanofilm and nanodots were synthesized without and with nitrogen in the reactive gases. When the carbon nanodots were prepared by electrochemical etching and hydrothermal methods, they required a long growth time (above 8h) and the special treatments such as the stirring, filtering, centrifugation and dry [9,12]. In comparison with the electrochemical etching and hydrothermal methods, the PEHFCVD method simplifies the preparation procedure and lowers the growth time because the carbon nanodots can be directly synthesized for 15min by PEFCVD.

The PL and EFE properties of carbon nanofilm and nanodots were studied. The results show that the PL bands of carbon nanodots have a blue shift relative to the carbon nanofilm while the carbon nanofilm can emit higher current density relative to the carbon nanodots. Combined with the theory related to sputtering, the formation of carbon nanofilm and nanodots was studied. According to the transition mechanism and the structures of carbon nanofilm and nanodots, the PL and EFE results were analyzed. In this paper, we report the results on the comparative study of formation, structure, photoluminescence and electron field emission of carbon nanofilm and nanodots.

The carbon nanofilm and nanodots were synthesized in a PEHFCVD system, which is described in detail in Ref. [16]. Briefly, the reactive chamber includes both the filament and bias systems for the decomposition of reactive gases, the heating of substrate and the production of plasma, respectively. The filament system is constructed by three parallel tungsten filaments, which were heated by AC current to about 1800°C. The distance between the filaments and substrate was about 8mm. The temperature of filaments was so high that the silicon substrate was fast heated to above 800°C because of its exposure to the filaments. In the bias system, a DC power supply with constant current is applied. Both the anode and cathode of DC power supply are connected to the filaments and substrate, respectively, and the cathode is connected to the substrate through a molybdenum holder.

The synthesis process includes the treatment of silicon surface by plasma and the preparation of carbon nanomaterials. Because it is difficult to nucleate on a mirror-polished silicon surface, the silicon substrate was treated by N2–H2 plasma to produce the micro-pits on the surface of silicon for lowering the energy required for carbon nucleation [17]. After the silicon substrate was placed into the reactive chamber, the chamber was evacuated to a pressure of lower than 2Pa. Then, nitrogen and hydrogen gases were inlet into the chamber and the pressure was adjusted to about 2×103 Pa. Under this pressure, the filaments were heated to 1800°C. Simultaneously, the silicon substrate was fast heated to about 850°C. At this moment, the bias power supply was turned on to produce plasma. The bias current was set to 140mA to treat the silicon surface for 5min. After the silicon surface was treated, the bias power supply was turned off and methane was introduced into the chamber. Once the flow rates of reactive gases were adjusted according to the preparation conditions, the bias power supply was turned on again and the bias current was set to 140mA to deposit the carbon nanomaterials for 15min. In this work, two specimens A and B were prepared and the preparation parameters are summarized in Table 1. Through experiment, we find that the method has a good reproducibility for the preparation of carbon nanofilms and nanodots and have used the carbon nanofilms and nanodots as the seeds to successfully synthesize the graphene flakes (reported in other papers).

The morphologies and composition of specimens were investigated by a Hitachi S-4800 field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (operated at 15KV), a HR-800 micro-Raman spectroscopy using the 532nm line of semiconductor laser and a ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using an Al Kα X-ray source, respectively. The PL measurements of specimens were carried out in a SPEX 1403 Ramalog system, where the 325nm line of He–Cd laser was used as an excitation source.

The EFE characteristics of specimens were studied using a diode configuration in a high-vacuum system of ~10−5 Pa. The cathode and anode of diode configuration were composed of the specimen and a mirror-polished silicon wafer, respectively. Both the electrodes were isolated using the glass fibers with a diameter of 125μm. During the process of measurement, the voltage was changed from 1 to 980V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structures and composition of carbon nanofilm and nanodots
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the FESEM images of specimens A and B. As shown in Fig. 1, the carbon nanofilm and nanodots were formed without and with nitrogen, respectively. From Fig. 1, one can see that the specimen A is carbon film composed of carbon nanoparticles, while the specimen B is composed of the isolated carbon nanodots.

Fig. 2 is the Raman spectra of specimens A and B. The spectrum (a) shows two main Raman peaks centered at about 1354 and 1603cm−1 while the spectrum (b) shows three main Raman peaks centered at about 1184, 1366 and 1610cm−1, respectively. The two peaks at about 1354 and 1603cm−1 in the spectrum (a) are assigned as the D and G peaks of carbon nanomaterials, and the presence of peak at about 1603cm−1 is due to the presence of carbon nanocrystalline graphitic features [1]. For the two peaks at about 1366 and 1610cm−1 in the spectrum (b), they are the D and G peaks of carbon nanomaterials. In comparison with the G and D peaks in the spectrum (a), the two peaks at about 1610 and 1366cm−1 have a great shift toward the high frequency, which results from the nitrogen incorporation and the increase of defects in the carbon nanodots, respectively [18,19]. The peak at about 1184cm−1 in the spectrum (b) is associated with the Raman scattering of silicon oxide [20]. Although the peak at about 1184cm−1 appears in the Raman spectrum of amorphous carbon films and single-walled carbon nanotubes [21,22], it can be generated in the amorphous carbon films by the ultraviolet irradiation [21] and there is a corresponding Raman peak to present at about 1139cm−1 under the irradiation of visible light [22]. From Fig. 1(b), one can see that there is the exposed surface on silicon substrate. Simultaneously, the Raman peaks of silicon oxide appear at 450, 800 and 1050–1200cm−1 [20]. According to our measurement condition and the Raman spectrum, the peak at about 1184cm−1 results from the Raman scattering of silicon oxide.

Fig. 3 is the XPS spectra of specimens A and B. As shown in Fig. 3, both the spectra reveal the peaks at about 102.3, 153.3, 284.8, 532.5 and 978.4eV, which are attributed to Si 2p, Si 2s, C 1s, O 1s and C KLL, respectively [23]. For the peak at about 398.2eV in the spectrum (b), it is the XPS peak of N 1s. The content of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen obtained from measurement is shown in Table 2.

Fig. 4 shows the C 1s, O 1s and N 1s core level XPS spectra of specimens A and B to study the chemical binding states of C, O and N. Due to the asymmetric shapes, the spectra should be composed of multi-peaks, thus they are fitted using a standard XPS peak fitting procedure after a Shirley background subtraction. As shown in Fig. 4(a) and (b), the C 1s core level XPS spectra are fitted by three peaks at about 284.8, 285.0 and 286.8eV for the specimen A and two peaks at about 284.8 and 286.1eV for the specimen B, respectively. The peaks at about 284.8 and 286.1eV are attributed to the sp 2 C–C and C–N bonds, respectively [23]. The peak at about 285.0eV is related to the sp 3 carbon [24]. For the peak at about 286.8eV, it is associated with the binding of carbon with oxygen [25,26]. In Fig. 4(c) and (d), the O 1s core level XPS spectra are fitted by two peaks at 532.2 and 532.8eV for the specimen A and one peak at about 532.6eV for the specimen B, respectively. The peak at about 532.2eV results from the COC group [23], while the two peaks at about 532.6 and 532.8eV are linked to the OCC group [27]. For the N 1s core level XPS spectra in Fig. 4(e) and (f), they are decomposed into two peaks at about 401.5 and 407.2eV for the specimen A and two peaks at about 398.2 and 400.0eV for the specimen B, respectively. The two peaks at about 401.5 and 407.2eV are associated with N–H and NO bonds, respectively [28], while the two peaks at about 398.2 and 400.0eV originate from the sp 3 and sp 2 CN bonds, respectively [23,29].

As shown in Fig. 4(e), there is no formation of CN bonds, while there are the NH and NO bonds to be formed. The formation of N–H and NO bonds originates from the residual nitrogen during the pre-treatment of silicon substrate. It is the reason that Table 2 shows few of nitrogen in the specimen A.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The formation of carbon nanofilm and nanodots
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. 1, the carbon nanofilm and nanodots are formed without and with nitrogen, which are related to the etching of nitrogenous ions. Due to the production of plasma, the reactive gases are ionized by plasma into various hydrocarbon, nitrogenous and hydrogen ions [16,30]. Among these ions, the nitrogenous and hydrogen ions are the etching ions and the hydrocarbon ions are used for the formation of carbon nanomaterials. Simultaneously, the plasma results in the formation of an electric field near the substrate [17]. The nitrogenous and hydrogen ions gain energy from the electric field to bombard the carbon nanomaterials. As a result, there appear the sputtering-etching effects on the carbon nanomaterials during the growth of carbon nanomaterials. According to Sigmund׳s theory of sputtering, the sputtering yield Y can be expressed by
(1)
Y =
3
4
π
2
4 α
m
i
m
C
(
m
i
+
m
C
)
2
W
W
B
( cos
θ )
− f
,
where m
i and m
C are the mass of ion and carbon atom, W and W
B are the energy of ion and the binging energy between two carbon atoms, θ is the incident angle, and α and f are the constant depending on the ion energy and m
C /m
i , respectively [31]. Here, f is equal to 1 for hydrogen ions and f is greater than 1 for nitrogenous ions [31]. After m
i in Eq. (1) is replaced by the mass of hydrogen and nitrogen ions, we can obtain that the sputtering yield of hydrogen ion is about 13 times lower than that of nitrogen ion. According to the growth conditions shown in Table 1, only hydrogen was employed for the specimen A, while the ratio of nitrogen to hydrogen is 4/5 for the specimen B, thus there are more nitrogenous ions to be produced in the growth process of specimen B. Consequently, the sputtering-etching effects are strong during the growth of the specimen B so that a number of carbon atoms are sputtered and the carbon nanodots are formed. However, the sputtering is weak during the growth of specimen A, which indicates that the deposition of hydrocarbon chemical radicals dominates the growth process. In this case, the carbon nanofilm is formed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence of carbon nanofilm and nanodots
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 is the PL spectra of carbon film and nanodots at room temperature, which are similar with the results of amorphous carbon nanotips [4]. As shown in Fig. 5, the PL spectrum (a) shows two PL bands centered at about 411 and 513nm, while the spectrum (b) shows two PL bands centered at about 405 and 504nm. These data indicate that every PL band of carbon nanodots has a blue shift relative to the corresponding PL band of carbon nanofilm. From Fig. 5, we can obtain the intensity of every PL band and it is about 595, 1877, 546 and 1526 for the PL bands centered at bout 405, 504, 411 and 513nm, i.e., the intensity of PL bands in the spectrum (b) is stronger than that of the corresponding PL bands in the spectrum (a). From Fig. 1, one can see that the specimens A and B are composed of carbon nanodots, thus the PL generation can be considered to originate from carbon nanodots.

To date, the PL properties of carbon nanodots are extensively studied and the PL bands centered at about 411 and 513nm are observed [9,13,32,33], but the PL mechanism is quite different. Sun and Zhou et al. suggested that the PL generation of carbon nanodots is the consequence of surface passivation [13], while Ming and Ma et al. believed that the PL results from the geminate recombination of localized electron–hole pairs in sp 2 clusters [9]. However, the radiative recombination is generated in the small sp 2 clusters whose size is in a range of 0.94–1.15nm [10]. Fig. 4 indicates that there are the sp 3 carbon bonds in the specimens A and B, thus the Raman spectra in Fig. 2 are fitted to estimate the size of sp 2 carbon clusters [4]. The fitted Raman spectra are shown in Fig. 6. The positions, full width at half maximum and intensity of peaks are summarized in Table 3. The relation of the size of sp 2 carbon clusters with the intensity ratio of D–G peak is,
(2)
A
D
A
G
=
− 126 + 0.033 λ
l
where λ is the wavelength of excitation source, l is the size of sp 2 carbon clusters, and A
D and A
G are the intensities of D and G peaks, respectively [4]. After A
D and A
G in Eq. (2) are replaced by the intensity values of D2A, G2A, D2B and G2B in Table 3, we obtain the size of aromatic sp 2 carbon clusters and it is about 4.9 and 5.9nm for the specimens A and B, respectively. Here the intensity values of D2A, G2A, D2B and G2B are used to calculate the size of sp 2 carbon clusters because the radiative recombination occurs in the aromatic sp 2 carbon clusters and the D2A, G2A, D2B and G2B peaks are corresponding to D and G peaks of aromatic sp 2 carbon clusters [4]. According to the size of aromatic sp 2 carbon clusters, it is impossible to generate the PL bands through the radiative recombination of electron–hole pairs for our specimens. Thus, the generation of PL bands is related to other mechanism. Iwano and Kittaka et al. studied the cathodoluminescence of amorphous carbon nitride and proposed the interband transition mechanism. They claimed that the transition energy more than 2eV occurred between π ⁎ conduction and π valance band, or σ ⁎ conduction and lone-pair (LP) valance band [11]. Fig. 4 shows the peaks related to the sp 2 C–C, sp 3 C–C, sp 2 C–N and sp 3 CN bonds, which means the formation of LP electrons [4]. As a result, the LP valence bands can be formed within the π band and between π and π ⁎ band for the sp 2 and sp 3 hybridized nitrogen atom, respectively [34,35], thus the generation of PL bands for our specimens can be interpreted by the interband transition.

For the PL bands centered at about 411, 513, 405 and 504nm, their corresponding energies are 3.02, 2.42, 3.07, and 2.47eV, respectively. In Ref. [36], it is found that the gap between π and π ⁎ band is ~3eV. According to Ref. [11], the PL band centered at about 504nm is attributed to the transition between the π ⁎ band and LP valence band related to the sp 3 CN bonds. The PL band centered at about 405nm can be attributed to the transition between the σ ⁎ and π band or between π ⁎ band and LP valence band related to the sp 2 C–N bonds. For the specimen A, the XPS spectra do not show the formation of CN bonds, i.e., there is no LP valence band to form in the specimen A. Thus, the PL bands centered at about 513 and 411nm are assigned to the transition between the π and π ⁎ band and the σ ⁎ conduction and π band, respectively. From the above analyses, the blue shift of PL bands of specimen B is due to the incorporation of nitrogen in the carbon nanodots.

Of course, it is important for the surface passivation to the PL generation. If the surfaces of carbon nanodots are not passivated, there will be a number of dangling bonds on the surface of carbon nanodots so that the PL is quickly quenched by these paramagnetic dangling bonds which act as the nonradiative recombination centers [37]. However, the generation of PL bands should be determined by the interband rather than the surface passivation.

For the difference in the intensity of PL bands, it is related to the difference in the size of carbon nanodots. As shown in Fig. 1, the carbon nanodots are diversified. The size of carbon particles in the specimen A is 16~26nm for the small carbon nanodots and 42~58nm for the large carbon nanodots. For the specimen B, the size is 13~17nm for the small carbon nanodots and 37~68nm for the large carbon nanodots. These data imply the average size of carbon nanodots in the specimen A is larger than that in the specimen B, thus the specimen B has a wider band-gap than the specimen A [10]. Due to the wide band-gap, the specimen B has a high PL efficiency [4]. As a result, the specimen B generates stronger PL bands than the specimen A. In addition, Fig. 6 shows that the low intensity of blue light (405 and 411nm PL bands) relative to the green light (504 and 513nm PL bands), which can restrict some applications of carbon-based nanomaterials such as the development of white light emission devices. For the expensive applications of carbon nanodots, their size can be reduced via adjusting the growth conditions such as reducing the flow rate of methane and raising the flow rate of nitrogen to increase the intensity of blue light because the intensity of blue light can be improved by the increase of bandgap [4] and the bandgap is increased with the size reduction of sp 2 carbon clusters [10].

ce:section.ce:section-title: EFE properties of carbon film and nanodots
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 7 is the curves of current density versus electric field (J–E) of the specimens A and B, which the insets are the corresponding Fowler–Nordhelm (F–N) curves. From Fig. 7, we obtain the turn-on field and they are about 3.76 and 5.2V/μm for the specimens A and B according to the definition of the turn-on field (i.e., the turn-on field is defined the field when the current density reaches at 1μA/cm2 [38]). In addition, Fig. 7 also shows that the current density is above 100μA/cm2 and 46μA/cm2 at a field of 7.8V/μm for the specimens A and B, respectively. For the carbon nanomaterials, their EFE properties are analyzed by F–N theory, which the F–N equation is expressed by,
(3)
J =
1.56
( β E )
2
ϕ
exp
(
−
6.83 ×
10
3
ϕ
3 / 2
β E
)
,
where ϕ is the work function, β is the field enhancement factor, J is the current density (μA/cm2), E is the applied electric field (V/μm) [38,39]. According to Eq. (3), the slope of F–N curve is,
(4)
s = −
6.83 ×
10
3
ϕ
3 / 2
β
Eq. (4) indicates that the F–N curve should be composed of only one straight line if the work function and field enhancement factor are defined. However, Fig. 7 shows two or three straight lines, which is related to the diversification of carbon nanodots.

As shown in Fig. 1, the carbon nanodots are diversified. The difference in the size of carbon nanodots results in the difference in their band gaps [10] so that there is a difference in the work function for them [16]. Because the expansion of band-gap with the size reduction makes Fermi level move toward valence band, the small carbon nanodots have a high work function and the large carbon nanodots have a low work function [16].

From Fig. 1, one can see that the carbon nanodots in the specimen A are the polyhedral and ellipsoid-like particles while the carbon nanodots in the specimen B are sphere-like particles. The results in Ref. [40] indicate that the spherical particles have a definite field enhancement factor while the ellipsoid particles have the shape-dependant field enhancement factor. In addition, the sharp angle of polyhedral particles can lead to a very high field enhancement factor [41].

According to the above analyses, the diversification of carbon nanodots results in the difference in the work function and field enhancement factor. According to Eq. (3), the large carbon nanodots with an irregular shape firstly emit electrons due to their low work function and high field enhancement factor, and then the small carbon nanodots emit electrons. Thus, Fig. 7 shows that the F–N curves of specimens A and B are composed of three straight and two straight lines.

Comparing Fig. 1(a) with Fig.1(b), we can find that the specimen B is composed of a number of small sphere-like carbon nanodots with the size of 13~17nm (shown in Fig. 8) and the specimen A contains a great number of polyhedral carbon nanodots with the size of ~25nm. Thus, the specimen A exhibits a lower turn-on field than the specimen B. From Fig. 1 it can be found that the specimen A has a higher density of carbon nanodots than the specimen B, which implies that the specimen A has more carbon nanodots for the electron emission. Thus, Fig. 7 shows a high current density for the specimen A compared with the specimen B.

The inset in Fig. 7(a) shows that some points deviate from the straight line, which is related to the desorption of oxygen. From Fig. 7, the specimen A can generate high current so that oxygen absorbed on surface of specimen is desorbed by the Joule-heat effect during electron emission [42]. As a result, the desorbed oxygen is ionized by the field to result in the deviation of some points from the straight line.

Although the carbon nanofilms can easily emit electrons for the development the microelectronic devices, their thickness must be appropriate. During experiment, we found that the carbon nanofilms were easily separated from the silicon substrates when the thickness is up to 400nm, thus their thickness should be well controlled for their applications.

Here we should mention that PEHFCVD can be used to synthesize other important nanomaterials such as single-walled carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and multi-walled carbon nanotubes because they are the important materials of microelectronic devices. The results of Ref. [43] have verified the synthesis of multi-walled CNTs by PEHFCVD. For the single-walled CNTs, they can be synthesized by PEHFCVD if the appropriate catalyst is selected.

In summary, carbon nanofilm and nanodots were grown by PEHFCVD under different conditions and their structural and compositional properties were investigated by FESEM, micro-Raman spectroscopy and XPS. The formation of carbon nanofilm and nanodots is the consequence of different sputtering-etching effects. The PL and EFE properties were studied in a SPEX 1403 Ramalog system and high-vacuum system of ~105 Pa, respectively. The PL results show that the carbon nanodots and nanoflm generate the PL bands centered at about 405, 411, 504 and 513nm, which originate from different band transitions. The strong PL bands for the carbon nanodot specimen are related to the small size of carbon nanodots. The EFE results show that the F–N curves are composed of two or three straight lines, which result from the diversification of carbon nanodots. The high current density emitted from the carbon nanofilm is associated to the increase in the number of carbon nanodots. These results can enrich our knowledge about carbon-based nanomaterials and are important to fabricate the carbon-based solid nanodevices in the field of optoelectronics.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Beijing Nova Program (Z121103002512017), the Foundation for the Author of National Excellent Doctoral Dissertation of PR China, National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (Grant nos. 11274029, 11074017, 10904001 and 51032002), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, and Program for New Century Excellent Talents in Fujian Province University (NCETFJ).
